1: 'Now muster your troops, O daughter of troops; siege is laid against us; with a rod they strike the judge of Israel on the cheek.' The verse describes the present siege crisis — the humiliation of Israel's judge (king) being struck on the cheek. The 'striking on the cheek' was a gesture of supreme contempt and legal degradation in the ancient Near East (cf. 1 Kings 22:24; Job 16:10). Against this backdrop of royal humiliation, the announcement of a new shepherd-king from Bethlehem (Micah 5:2) is all the more dramatic.
2: 'But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.' The most famous verse in Micah: the birthplace of the coming ruler. Beit-Lechem Efrata distinguishes this Bethlehem from another in Zebulun (Joshua 19:15). 'Too little among the clans of Judah' (tzair lihyot be'alfei Yehudah) — not a large tribal center but a village. Yet from this small Davidic hometown the ruler will come — inverting human expectations of where power originates. Motza'otav mikedem miyemei olam ('whose going forth is from of old, from ancient days') is cited in the NT for the pre-existence of Christ (Matthew 2:6; John 7:42).
3: 'Therefore he shall give them up until the time when she who is in labor has given birth; then the rest of his brothers shall return to the people of Israel.' The interim period before the ruler's coming — Israel in labor pains. The woman in labor connects back to Micah 4:9-10 (daughter Zion writhing in labor). The period of exile or occupation is the gestation before the messianic birth. 'The rest of his brothers shall return' — a diaspora-gathering associated with the messianic advent.
4: 'And he shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of YHWH, in the majesty of the name of YHWH his God. And they shall dwell secure, for now he shall be great to the ends of the earth.' The coming ruler is a shepherd (ra'ah) — the primary royal metaphor in the ancient Near East and Israel. His strength is YHWH's strength; his majesty is YHWH's name — he rules by divine authorization, not his own power. His greatness extends 'to the ends of the earth' (ad-afsei aretz) — universal scope that goes beyond David's historical kingdom.
5: 'And he shall be their peace. When the Assyrian comes into our land and treads in our palaces, we will raise against him seven shepherds and eight princes of men.' 'He shall be their peace' (vehayah zeh shalom) — the ruler himself, not just his rule, is shalom. The 'seven shepherds and eight princes' is a numerical saying (x / x+1 pattern common in Hebrew wisdom poetry: Proverbs 6:16, Amos 1:3) indicating sufficiency — enough military leadership to repel any enemy. The immediate application is anti-Assyrian, but the ruler-as-peace principle transcends the historical referent.
6: 'They shall shepherd the land of Assyria with the sword, and the land of Nimrod at its entrances; and he shall deliver us from the Assyrian when he comes into our land and treads within our border.' Assyria/Nimrod (Babylon — see Genesis 10:8-12; Nimrod's cities include Nineveh and Babel) are the specific imperial enemies. The coming ruler will reverse the threat: instead of Assyria threatening Israel, Israel's shepherds will shepherd Assyria. The oracle operates on two levels — historical (8th-century Assyrian crisis) and eschatological (permanent defeat of all imperial enemies).
7: 'Then the remnant of Jacob shall be in the midst of many peoples like dew from YHWH, like showers on the grass, which delay not for a man nor wait for the children of man.' The remnant as blessing to the nations — like dew and rain that come not by human effort but by YHWH's provision. The dew simile appears in Psalms 133:3 (fraternal unity) and Genesis 27:28 (Isaac's blessing for Jacob). The remnant is not merely preserved Israel but a life-giving presence among the nations — a second reading of the Abrahamic 'blessing to all nations' promise (Genesis 12:3).
8: 'And the remnant of Jacob shall be among the nations, in the midst of many peoples, like a lion among the beasts of the forest, like a young lion among the flocks of sheep, which, when it goes through, treads down and tears in pieces, and there is none to deliver.' The same remnant described in Micah 5:7 as gentle dew is now a lion among the nations. The two images are complementary: blessing to those who receive the remnant, judgment to those who oppose it. The lion imagery is messianic in Genesis 49:9 (Judah as lion's cub).
9: 'Your hand shall be lifted up over your adversaries, and all your enemies shall be cut off.' The warfare oracle continues — Israel's hand lifted over enemies. This is the covenant-victory blessing of Deuteronomy 28:1, 7. The oracle moves from the lion image to direct address of the remnant/Israel, assuring them of ultimate military reversal over all enemies.
10: 'And in that day, declares YHWH, I will cut off your horses from among you and will destroy your chariots.' YHWH's purification of Israel begins with its military hardware — horses and chariots. This is the Deuteronomic prohibition applied eschatologically: Deuteronomy 17:16 explicitly forbids the Israelite king from multiplying horses, and Isaiah 31:1-3 condemns reliance on Egyptian cavalry. The coming kingdom will be secured by YHWH's power, not military technology.
11: 'And I will cut off the cities of your land and throw down all your strongholds.' The fortified cities — symbols of military self-reliance and also centers of the socioeconomic injustice Micah condemns — will be removed. The purification removes both military (Micah 5:10-11) and cultic (Micah 5:12-14) sources of false security and idolatry.
12: 'And I will cut off sorceries from your hand, and you shall have no more tellers of fortunes.' Divination and sorcery (keshafim, me'onautim) are Deuteronomic abominations (Deuteronomy 18:10-12) — the attempt to control the future through magical means rather than trusting YHWH. The eschatological purification eliminates the spiritual adultery alongside the physical idolatry. True peace requires purity.
13: 'And I will cut off your carved images and your pillars from among you, and you shall bow down no more to the work of your hands.' Sacred standing stones (matsevot) were originally acceptable worship markers (Genesis 28:18; Micah 31:45) but became associated with Baalism and were repeatedly condemned in the reform texts (2 Kings 3:2; Micah 10:27). Their elimination in the coming age removes the temptation to worship created things over the Creator.
14: 'And I will root out your Asherah poles from among you and destroy your cities.' The Asherah poles were wooden cult symbols associated with the Canaanite mother goddess, condemned throughout the Deuteronomic reform literature (1 Kings 14:15; 2 Kings 21:3; Micah 23:6). Their removal completes the purification of the land. 'Destroy your cities' — literally 'your enemies' in some textual traditions — may refer to enemy cities or, following some manuscripts, the cities of the idolatrous.
15: 'And in anger and wrath I will execute vengeance on the nations that did not obey.' The purification of Israel (Micah 5:10-14) is paralleled by judgment on the disobedient nations. The balance of Micah 5: the remnant will be both blessing (Micah 5:7) and judgment (Micah 5:8-9) among the nations; YHWH will purify his own people while executing vengeance on those who rejected him. The chapter ends with YHWH's wrath as boundary on the universal reign of peace described at the chapter's opening.
